text: Robotic anterior exoneration in females. Tips and tricks. Radical cystectomy is the gold standard for treatment of muscle invasive bladder cancer. Females are less likely than men to experience bladder cancer by a factor of four to one and therefore less frequently undergo radical cystectomy. Generally, urologists are less familiar with female pelvic anatomy and are more comfortable with radical cystectomy in men than women. With the increased utilization of robotic radical cystectomy, females are potentially able to benefit even more with a truly minimally invasive operation. as the specimen can be extracted through the vagina, resulting in no fascial closure.

From September, to October, we have performed seventy-five robotic radical cystectomies, with ten being in females. In the following video, we demonstrate and highlight some tips and tricks for robotic anterior exoneration in females.

The surgeon places ports similarly to male patients and identifies the gonadal veins bilaterally, ligating them high to form a single vein. The gonadal vein is dissected caudally towards the ovaries. The ureters are dissected caudally, crossing beneath the uterine arteries, which are then ligated in preparation for hysterectomy. The uterus, fallopian tubes, ovaries, and round ligaments are grasped, and the peritoneal reflection posterior to the broad ligament is taken down to the ureters.

The broad ligament is visible, containing the round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovary. The surgeon uses the fallopian tubes to lift the uterus. A sponge stick is inserted into the vagina to identify the cervix, ensuring it is not mistakenly placed in the rectum. The vagina is incised with electrocautery, revealing the anterior cervix. The dissection is potentially bloody due to the vascularization of the vagina. The ureters are clipped distally, and the lateral sidewalls are cleaned to expose the endopelvic fascia, extending laterally and cephalad to remove maximum tissue. The lateral perivesticle tissue is removed to prevent obstruction during the operation.

The surgeon exposes the endopelvic fascia on the right side and sends off extra tissue. The uterus is elevated to reveal the vaginal wall, and a vessel sealer device is used to take down the lateral pedicles, avoiding rectal injury. The vessel sealer performs a partial vaginotomy, rolling medially to spare vaginal tissue. The bladder is dropped, and anterior dissection exposes the dorsal venous complex, which is cauterized with bipolar and dissected out. The catheter is pulled to identify the bladder neck, aiding in urethra identification. An incision is made lateral to the urethra, which is visible.

The surgeon carefully creates a plane between the urethra and the anterior vaginal wall, circumferentiating the urethra. The anterior vaginal wall is dissected to form a vaginal flap that avoids the urethra. A total urethrectomy is performed, with the urethra being pulled into the body and dissected in the appropriate plane. After transecting the urethra, a clip is placed on the Foley catheter to maintain the balloon. The separate urethral meatus and anterior vaginal flap are visible. An endocatch bag is inserted through the vagina, and the specimen is placed inside for retrieval. The assistant removes the endocatch bag and specimen through the vagina, eliminating the need for fascial incision and closure.

The surgeon carefully separates the posterior vagina from the posterior peritoneum, avoiding injury to the rectum. A peritoneal flap is brought up to cover the vaginal closure, which is performed in a clamshell fashion using two separate zero V-lock sutures. The lateral aspects are sutured and tied together in the middle. A three-oh V-lock suture is used to close the separate urethral opening, with the peritoneal flap aiding in closing the vaginal suture line to prevent fistula formation.
The surgeon sews the peroneal flap laterally to the arcanus tendinus on both sides, completing the dissection with the peroneal flap visible.
